context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about “development as freedom” and rights-based development planning, and I’m honestly not fully sure how to connect those ideas. Could you help me figure out the main argument and maybe what tensions I should discuss?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Before we map out anything, let me ask you this: when you hear the phrase 'development as freedom,' whose work does that make you think of, and what do you already understand it to mean? Start there and we'll build from what you know.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lay out the main argument and tensions for them, which would be doing the intellectual work on their behalf. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover their own entry point. I'll start by asking them what they already understand about 'development as freedom' to assess their baseline and let them build from there. I'll keep it short and focused.

exchanges.student: I think it’s Amartya Sen, but I’m a little fuzzy on the exact meaning. My rough understanding is that development isn’t just economic growth, but also whether people actually have real choices and opportunities in their lives.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good starting point — you've captured the core shift Sen is making. Now push that a little further: what kinds of 'real choices and opportunities' are we talking about? Can you think of some concrete examples of what counts as freedom in Sen's sense, and what might block it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of Sen's core idea. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to go deeper by asking what specific kinds of freedoms or capabilities Sen talks about, which will help them develop more precise language for their reflection. I won't give them the answer but will nudge them to think more concretely.

exchanges.student: Maybe things like being able to get education, healthcare, work, and participate politically? And blocks would be poverty, discrimination, bad government, or maybe even laws that technically exist but don’t help people in practice.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those examples work well. Now here's the key question the prompt is actually asking: compared to measuring development purely by GDP or economic growth, what does this broader definition *add*? Why might it matter for a planner to think this way instead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a good concrete understanding of Sen's capabilities/freedoms. Now I should push them toward the assignment's actual question: the 'added value' of this framing. Rather than telling them, I'll ask them to compare it to a narrower definition of development to help them articulate why this framing matters differently.

exchanges.student: I think it adds a way to judge development by people’s actual lived conditions, not just national income. So a country could have growth, but if a lot of people still can’t access school, healthcare, safety, or political voice, then calling that “development” seems incomplete. For planning, I guess that means policies should focus more on expanding people’s capabilities instead of only increasing output.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong articulation of the added value. Now let's move to the next part of the prompt: how does this connect to *rights-based* development planning? Is there a difference between saying 'people should have capabilities' and saying 'people have a *right* to development'? What does framing it as a right add — or complicate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid progress and has articulated the core added value well. Now I should push them toward the second and third parts of the prompt: the relationship between 'development as freedom' and rights-based planning, and the question of who gets to exercise the right to development. These are the parts they haven't addressed yet and are crucial for the reflection.

exchanges.student: I’m not totally sure, but I think “capabilities” sounds more like what people are able to do, while “rights” sounds more legal and political. So rights-based planning might make governments more accountable, since it’s not just a good outcome but something people can claim. But I’m also guessing it complicates things because rights can be unevenly enforced, or they can stay abstract if the state is weak.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction — the accountability dimension is exactly what rights-based framing adds. Now the prompt asks something even thornier: *who gets to exercise* the right to development? Think about this at different scales — individuals, communities, states, international actors. Who actually holds that right in practice, and who decides?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely insightful distinction between capabilities and rights, and has identified both the added value and a key tension. I should briefly affirm this and then push them toward the final and most politically charged part of the prompt: 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' This is where power, legitimacy, and external actors come in — core course themes.

exchanges.student: This is where I get a little stuck. My first thought is individuals should hold it, but I can also see why states claim a right to development in international politics, especially if they’re dealing with unequal global structures. I’m not sure how to talk about communities versus states without getting messy.
exchanges.tutor: The messiness *is* the argument — don't try to clean it up too fast. Here's a question to help you think it through: in developing countries specifically, what happens when individual rights and state claims to development *conflict*? Who tends to win, and what does that tell us about power and legitimacy in development planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is productively grappling with a real tension rather than just being confused. The messiness they feel is actually the substance of the argument. I should validate that this tension is real and meaningful, then nudge them to think about what the course themes — power, legitimacy, external actors — suggest about why this question is contested. I shouldn't resolve it for them.

exchanges.student: I think the state often wins, especially when it says a project is for national development, like infrastructure or industrialization. But that can override local communities or individuals who lose land, housing, or political voice, which makes the “right to development” sound selective. So maybe that shows development planning is tied to power: whoever has legitimacy and institutional authority gets to define whose freedom counts.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really strong insight and it connects directly to the course's core themes. Now bring it back to the prompt: given everything you've just said, does framing development as freedom *help* or *hinder* planning — or both? What's your honest argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated what is arguably the central critical insight of the entire reflection — that development planning is tied to power and that rights-based framing doesn't automatically resolve whose freedoms count. This is excellent progress. Now I should push them to connect this back to the prompt's first question about whether this framing 'helps or hinders' development planning — they haven't explicitly addressed the 'hinders' side yet. That will round out their argument.

exchanges.student: I think it does both. It helps because it pushes planning to care about human well-being, agency, and justice instead of treating growth as enough, but it can hinder planning if the idea stays too broad or if states use the language of development or rights selectively. I’m leaning toward arguing that it’s valuable, but only if planners pay attention to power, accountability, and who actually gets included.
exchanges.tutor: You now have a real argument with genuine nuance — that's good work. You've covered all four parts of the prompt. It's time to start drafting. As you write, make sure each part of the prompt gets addressed. What's your opening move going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a coherent and nuanced argument that addresses all parts of the prompt. We've had about 7 exchanges and they have the intellectual substance for their reflection. This is a good moment to push them to start writing, since continued discussion risks over-scaffolding. I should affirm their progress and prompt them to begin drafting, reminding them of the word count and what they need to cover.

exchanges.student: I think I’d open by saying Sen redefines development away from GDP and toward the expansion of substantive freedoms, and that this is useful because it gives planning a more human-centered standard. Then I’d probably say that rights-based planning builds on that idea by turning freedom into a political claim, but it also raises conflicts over power and who gets to define development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid structure. Now stop planning and start writing — the 700-800 word limit is tight, so every sentence needs to carry weight. Come back with a draft and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear roadmap and is ready to write. I should encourage them to start drafting rather than continuing to plan verbally, since more discussion risks replacing the writing itself. I'll give a brief affirmation and remind them of the word count constraint, then send them off to write.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try a draft, but I may need help tightening it after because I tend to get repetitive.
exchanges.tutor: Just a heads-up: I won't edit or rewrite for you — that's your job. What I can do is ask you questions that help *you* identify where your argument repeats or loses focus. Go write the draft first, then bring it back.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pre-emptively asking for editing help, which falls outside my role — I should not edit, polish, or suggest language for the student. I should set that boundary clearly but encouragingly, and redirect them to the task of drafting.
